presentations.title: Board ERP Readout
presentations.objective: Secure board alignment on the recommended NetSuite implementation posture, the operating model to run the program, and the first 90-day mobilization plan required to protect the Jan 1, 2027 go-live.
narrative: Platform selection is no longer the hard part; execution model quality now determines whether Abivax gets a controlled implementation or a noisy one.
The proposed posture is NetSuite as platform and likely builder, with KPMG running day-to-day PMO and local coordination in France.
CFGI was considered for implementation and PMO support, but the current working recommendation is KPMG for PMO and related governance support.
The board discussion should lock operating model, resource posture, governance rhythm, risk guardrails, and the boundary between Phase 1 core ERP scope and broader transformation work.
keyMessages: Recommend NetSuite delivery with KPMG PMO oversight and France-based coordination.
Main board risk is execution failure through weak governance, unclear ownership, or overloaded internal teams.
The next 90 days must lock owners, cadence, staffing, and commercial closure rather than reopen platform debate.
Phase 1 should stay focused on core ERP pillars while adjacent items like TMS strategy, FP&A uplift, and Excel-heavy reporting automation are framed as parallel gates or later transformation waves.

dataRequests.topic: KPMG PMO scope and authority model
dataRequests.ask: Convert the March 6 PMO walk-through into a crisp board-ready statement of PMO scope, decision rights, local coverage in France, and interaction model with NetSuite.

dataRequests.topic: Implementation role split and internal staffing map
dataRequests.ask: Name the mobilized functions across Finance, IT, PMO, and vendor delivery with practical ownership for the first 90 days.
Condense current NetSuite commercials, implementation economics, and open contracting items into a board-ready summary with Year 1 and run-rate framing.
Explicitly include a board-level risk and mitigation slide reflecting ERP delay, overrun, and governance failure scenarios, per Didier follow-up.

slidePlan.title: Recommendation: Lock the Delivery Model Now
slidePlan.notes: Lead with the recommended operating model, not the process history.
content: Recommendation: proceed with NetSuite as selected platform and run implementation with KPMG day-to-day PMO oversight.
Reason: platform decision is largely made; execution quality, governance discipline, and local coordination now matter more than feature debate.
Board purpose: align on how the program will be run, not reopen vendor selection.

slidePlan.title: Economics: Pay for Control, Not for Complexity
slidePlan.notes: Must show implementation economics and open commercial items clearly.
content: NetSuite selected February 26, 2026 after structured selection process.
Current baseline in repo: Year 1 commercial package about EUR 650K, including build and annual run.
KPMG PMO proposal discussed March 6 at a flat fee of USD 350K initial proposal level.
Commercial focus now is implementation scope, PMO shape, and contract closure cadence ahead of April mobilization.

slidePlan.title: Operating Model: One Builder, One PMO, Clear Accountability
slidePlan.notes: Show Oracle / KPMG / Abivax role split cleanly.
content: Oracle / NetSuite: build, configure, and deliver the platform work.
KPMG PMO: run cadence, issue management, and on-the-ground coordination in France.
CFGI was considered as an implementation and PMO support option, but is not the current recommended PMO model.
Abivax leadership: keep design and prioritization ownership without becoming the day-to-day PMO.

slidePlan.title: First 90 Days: Protect the Go-Live by Controlling Scope
slidePlan.notes: Translate ambition into a short, named owner plan and make Phase 1 versus adjacent transformation boundaries explicit.
content: March: lock commercials, PMO authority, staffing commitments, and blueprint entry conditions.
April: launch blueprint and governance rhythm with named workstream owners.
May: resolve priority process and control design decisions before build accelerates.
Separate Phase 1 core ERP scope from parallel decision gates such as TMS path, treasury connectivity, and reporting / FP&A modernization.

slidePlan.title: Risk and Board Ask: Keep the Program Controlled
slidePlan.notes: Didier specifically asked for this emphasis in his March 3 follow-up.
content: Risk: weak implementation governance. Mitigation: KPMG PMO with explicit authority and weekly cadence.
Risk: internal capacity overload across Finance and IT. Mitigation: phase-based staffing commitments and role clarity.
Risk: unclear ownership between vendor, PMO, and Abivax. Mitigation: hard RACI before mobilization.
Risk: scope confusion between Phase 1 ERP and broader transformation needs. Mitigation: explicit boundary between core scope, parallel gates, and later transformation waves.
Board ask: endorse operating model, staffing posture, and risk management discipline for the next 90 days.

presentations.title: Audit Committee ERP Overview
presentations.objective: Introduce the ERP program to the Audit Committee at a high level — NetSuite selection, timeline, and financials. Establish awareness and directional endorsement. Controls deep-dive deferred to a future meeting.
narrative: This is a first introduction to the ERP program for the Audit Committee.
Content is deliberately high-level: what it is, when, and how much.
The ask is awareness and endorsement, not detailed control accountability — that comes later.
A controls-posture deep-dive will be scheduled once the implementation blueprint phase begins.
keyMessages: NetSuite selected over SAP. April 2026 mobilization. Jan 1, 2027 go-live.
This is a SOX-remediation program, not only a technology rollout.
Controls deep-dive briefing to follow at a future meeting.

artifacts.title: Camille KPMG Audit Committee Draft (Feb 26, 2026)
artifacts.notes: KPMG-produced source deck covering overview, timeline, and financial breakdown. Used as fact-check source for this presentation.

artifacts.title: NetSuite Commercial Review Notes (Mar 3, 2026)
artifacts.notes: Oracle/NetSuite call findings — confirmed contacts, 5 commercial issues, commercial figures validated.

slidePlan.title: ERP Program Overview — Audit Committee Briefing
slidePlan.notes: Dark navy title slide. Mike Markman as author. KPMG credited for selection-phase support.
content: Title slide only.

slidePlan.title: ERP Program Overview
slidePlan.notes: Why we're doing this / Why NetSuite / Program ownership. NetSuite selected Feb 26 over SAP.
content: Why: fragmented manual systems, inconsistent controls, not scalable for SEC filing.
Why NetSuite: 9-month implementation, SaaS-native, multi-GAAP, biotech track record.
Ownership: Mike Markman (ERP Lead), Hema Keshava (CFO Sponsor), NetSuite delivery team.

slidePlan.title: Implementation Timeline
slidePlan.notes: Mar 15 prep → Dec 27 go-live. 5 phases. 4 pre-condition cards.
content: Mar 15 – Apr 2026: Initiate & Design
May – Aug 2026: Configure & Build
Sep – Oct 2026: Test & Validate
Nov – Dec 15: Migration & UAT
Dec 27: GO-LIVE. Jan 1, 2027 non-negotiable.

slidePlan.title: Financial Overview
slidePlan.notes: Figures confirmed by KPMG. License/SOW in transit from Oracle.
content: 3-year total: €1,023,264
5-year total: €1,398,163
142 users, 29% discount, 392 man-days at €1,135.51/day

slidePlan.title: What We're Asking the Audit Committee
slidePlan.notes: Three asks: acknowledge program, endorse SOX frame, expect future controls deep-dive.
content: 01: Acknowledge program direction — NetSuite, April mobilization, Jan 1 go-live.
02: Endorse SOX/controls as primary program frame — not just a tech rollout.
03: Expect a controls deep-dive at a future meeting (Q3 2026, after blueprint phase).

presentations.title: Audit Committee ERP Controls Readout
presentations.objective: Establish audit committee confidence that ERP delivery will close priority control gaps with owner/date accountability.
narrative: Current finance operations rely on manual controls that do not scale to commercialization.
ERP program should be governed as a control-remediation program, not only a technology rollout.
Audit oversight should anchor on deficiency closure, evidence quality, and accountability cadence.
keyMessages: Manual process dependency is the primary control risk.
Control design and ownership decisions in 2026 determine audit readiness in 2027.
A dated remediation cadence is required now, not post-selection.

artifacts.title: IT Landscape Intro Notes (Jade/Benjamin)
artifacts.notes: Captured architecture, manual flows, and control pain points including Sage audit limitations.

artifacts.title: US Finance Intro Notes (Matt/Kimberly/Adrian)
artifacts.notes: Captured manual SEC reporting, IFRS bridge process, and US entity operational workflows.

artifacts.title: SAP and NetSuite Demo Feedback Decks
artifacts.notes: Provides gap list and control-relevant observations from demonstrations.

artifacts.title: France P2P Swimlane Visual Asset (v1)
artifacts.notes: Primary controls-oriented process visual for audit committee discussion of ERP-addressable P2P gaps and remediation path.

artifacts.title: Reporting / FP&A Data Flow Swimlane Visual Asset (v1)
artifacts.notes: Use to explain reporting bridge/manual control dependency and future-state control-by-design objectives.

artifacts.title: Controls / Auditability Swimlane Payload (v1)
artifacts.notes: Primary audit-facing controls process visual tying findings, remediation statuses, and ERP design checkpoints to the control program narrative.

slidePlan.title: Current Control Posture
content: Core P2P, reporting, and reconciliation activities remain heavily manual.
Control evidence and traceability are inconsistent across systems.
Legacy process design increases key-person and timing risk.
Current posture is not scalable for future filing scrutiny.

slidePlan.title: Priority Control Gaps
slidePlan.notes: Use controls/remediation swimlane (audit-art-6) to frame the current-state control program flow before discussing design principles and remediation roadmap.
content: P2P workflow approvals and evidence capture are fragmented.
Reporting and bridge processes are manual and consultant-dependent.
Integration monitoring/failure procedures are not standardized by owner.
US/France process divergence increases close and control complexity.

slidePlan.title: Target Control Model
slidePlan.notes: Use France P2P and Reporting/FP&A swimlane visuals (audit-art-4, audit-art-5) to show where control gaps and manual handoffs concentrate before detailing remediation expectations.
content: Embed approvals, role controls, and audit logging in-system.
Standardize feed ownership, monitoring, and failure escalation.
Create traceable flow from transaction initiation to filing support.
Align entity-level variations under a common control framework.

slidePlan.title: Remediation Governance
slidePlan.notes: Tie remediation roadmap to CFTI tracker/RCM signals and process visual stages so owners can see where ERP milestones support control closure.
content: Single deficiency register with severity, owner, due date, and status.
Weekly remediation governance with monthly audit committee rollup.
ERP milestones tied to control-closure checkpoints.
Escalation triggered on slippage against critical controls.

slidePlan.title: Audit Committee Ask
content: Endorse control-by-design as non-negotiable ERP principle.
Require owner/date accountability on top control gaps.
Approve monthly remediation reporting format and cadence.
Authorize fast-track support where controls threaten timeline.

presentations.title: ERP Program Priorities and Readiness (Living Executive Deck)
presentations.objective: Maintain a continuously updated executive-level program deck that clearly communicates ERP priorities (P2P, Reporting, Controls), readiness posture, major decisions, and delivery risk as the program evolves.
narrative: This is the canonical executive storyline for the ERP program and should stay aligned to the three ERP pillars.
The deck should be reusable for Didier, board, audit committee, and executive updates with audience-specific cuts.
Content should prioritize signal: current posture, changes since last update, decisions needed, and owner-dated actions.
keyMessages: NetSuite selection is complete; executive focus now shifts to negotiation discipline, implementation mobilization, and control-by-design execution.
The ERP program is a business operating-model and controls program, not only a system replacement.
Progress and risks should be reported consistently across P2P, reporting/data, and controls/auditability.
Executive governance quality and resourcing discipline determine delivery confidence more than vendor feature marketing.

dataRequests.ask: Quantify current P2P manual touchpoints, approval gaps, and monthly transaction volumes by entity (SA/LLC), including 1099-related process pain points.
Summarize current French GAAP -> IFRS/SEC bridge process, who does what (internal vs KPMG), cycle timing, and top failure points/manual dependencies.
Identify current SOX/control gaps, where remediation tracking lives today, and which deficiencies the ERP program is expected to address directly.
Confirm executive decision rights, workstream owners, meeting cadence, and expected internal capacity commitments by function (Finance/IT/KPMG/vendors).
Define standard visuals for recurring updates (pillar scorecard, timeline, risk heatmap, open decisions, owner-date tracker) and map source systems/notes feeding each visual.

artifacts.title: Controls / Auditability Swimlane Payload (v1)

slidePlan.title: ERP Program Mandate and Why It Matters Now
slidePlan.notes: Keep this short and executive-level. Anchor on ERP/SOX framing, Jan 1 2027 target, and why delay increases execution/control risk.
content: ERP/SOX is a 2026 finance priority with direct executive sponsorship.
The program is foundational to scaling finance and controls toward commercialization.
Near-term decisions should optimize execution certainty and governance, not feature theater.

slidePlan.title: Three ERP Priorities (P2P, Reporting, Controls)
slidePlan.notes: This should become the canonical recurring slide for exec audiences. Update the status indicators over time and keep terminology consistent across all decks.
content: P2P / Transaction Operations: modernize workflows, approvals, and payment control path.
Financial Reporting / Data Model: improve multi-entity, multi-GAAP, and management reporting capability.
SOX / Controls / Auditability: design traceable, testable controls and evidence retention into the future-state operating model.

slidePlan.title: Baseline Reality: Manual Complexity and Control Risk
slidePlan.notes: Use France P2P and Reporting/FP&A swimlane visuals (exec-art-4 and exec-art-5) with limited callouts from source-backed controls and current system handoffs. Keep it executive-level and avoid detailed control counts on this slide.
content: Current workflows are fragmented across multiple systems with manual handoffs.
Reporting and bridge processes remain heavily spreadsheet/KPMG dependent.
Control evidence and ownership are inconsistent, creating scaling and audit risk.

slidePlan.title: Program Readiness, Open Decisions, and Resourcing
slidePlan.notes: Use traffic-light status table as primary visual. Optional companion visual: controls/remediation swimlane (exec-art-6) to show where open issues sit in the operating process and where ERP checkpoints matter.
content: Separate completed selection work from unresolved delivery/governance decisions.
Show owner-dated actions required in next 30/60/90 days.
Explicitly call out capacity constraints and mitigation plan by function.

slidePlan.title: What Executives Should Review Each Update
slidePlan.notes: Define the recurring operating cadence for this deck: changes since last update, pillar status, key risks, decision requests, and next milestones.
content: Changes since last update (net-new evidence, decisions, risks).
Pillar status summary with confidence and major blockers.
Decision requests and milestone outlook with accountable owners.

presentations.title: Treasury Workstream Briefing
presentations.objective: Baseline treasury current state with Roxandra, identify open gaps in bank connectivity and cash management, and scope treasury module requirements for Phase 1 ERP.
narrative: Treasury is a sub-workstream under Record to Report, owned by Roxandra (FR) and Kimberly (US).
Current payment flow runs through four disconnected systems with manual handoffs.
Key decision: which banking integrations are in Phase 1 ERP scope — must be in contract before April mobilization.
keyMessages: Treasury scope must be defined before NetSuite contract is signed.
Bank connectivity requires IT integration work (Jade) that must be planned now.
Agicap future and EBICS authorization structure are the two biggest open questions.

dataRequests.ask: Step-by-step payment process from invoice approval through bank execution — who touches what, where.
How many accounts, which banks, FR vs US structure.
Is Agicap used for forecasting or just execution? Stays post-ERP or replaced by NetSuite?
Who are the authorized EBICS signers? What are the authorization thresholds?
How often are bank reconciliations performed? Where is evidence retained?

content: Title slide with Roxandra Sturdza, ERP Program, March 5, 2026.

slidePlan.title: Treasury in the ERP Program
content: R2R sub-workstream. Roxandra (FR), Kimberly (US). Go-live Jan 1, 2027. Mobilization Apr 2026.

slidePlan.title: Current State — What We Know
content: Sage → Trustpair → Agicap → Bank. 2 campaigns/month. 497 vendors. 4-day lead time.

slidePlan.title: Current Systems Landscape
content: Sage 100, Trustpair, Agicap, Globalcash, EBICS — functions and SOX relevance.

slidePlan.title: Controls & Compliance
content: FR-TR-12G (SoD), FR-TR-13G (SOC Review), plus payment-stage controls with status badges.

slidePlan.title: Open Questions for Roxandra
content: 9 discussion questions covering process, banks, Agicap, EBICS, reconciliation, intercompany, forecasting.

slidePlan.title: Key Decision & Next Steps
content: Treasury module scope for Phase 1. Owners: Mike, Roxandra, Jade. Deadline: before blueprint.
